coas.title: Option A — Fighter Intercept from Northern Vanguard
coas.summary: Scramble two fighters from Base North to intercept trk-h01 on the western approach, and two fighters from Highridge to cover trk-h02 on the eastern axis. UAV trk-h03 monitored only.
coas.reserve_posture: 9 aircraft remain: 2 at Base North, 1 at Highridge, 2 at Southern Redoubt (standby), 2 at Spear Point (alert). Both SAM batteries available. UAVs available for recon.
coas.estimated_outcome: High probability of intercepting both fighter tracks before they reach the Arktholm zone. Strong reserve maintained for follow-on threats.
assumptions: trk-h01 and trk-h02 maintain current heading and speed
No additional hostile tracks emerge in the next 60 seconds
Base North and Highridge runways remain operational
Fighter response time ~5 minutes to engagement envelope
coas.rationale: This is the balanced approach. By scrambling pairs from two different bases, we cover both threat axes without over-committing from any single base. Reserve at Southern Redoubt and Spear Point remains intact for a potential second wave. The main risk is that committing 4 fighters to 2 tracks leaves less margin if the tracks are a feint.

coas.title: Option B — SAM Engagement + Minimal Fighter Cover
coas.summary: Activate Firewatch SAM battery to cover the Arktholm approach. Scramble one fighter pair from Highridge as backup. Preserve all Northern Vanguard assets as reserve.
coas.reserve_posture: 11 aircraft remain: all 4 at Base North, 1 at Highridge, 2 UAVs, 2 at Southern Redoubt, 2 at Spear Point. SAM-Eastern available. Firewatch expends 2-4 missiles.
coas.estimated_outcome: SAM can engage trk-h01 if it enters the Firewatch coverage radius. Fighters cover the eastern axis. Lowest force commitment but relies on SAM effectiveness against a fast-mover at medium altitude.
assumptions: trk-h01 enters Firewatch SAM coverage envelope
SAM engagement probability adequate against medium-altitude fast mover
trk-h02 does not deviate to an uncovered approach
coas.rationale: Prioritizes force preservation. The SAM battery handles the primary threat while only 2 fighters are committed. This leaves the largest reserve for a potential second wave. The risk is that SAM engagement against a maneuvering fighter is less certain than a fighter intercept, and trk-h02's eastern approach is only covered by one fighter pair.

coas.title: Option C — Combined Response with Recon Screen
coas.summary: Fighter pair from Base North intercepts trk-h01. SAM Firewatch provides layered coverage. Fighter from Highridge covers trk-h02. Deploy one UAV forward for early warning on potential follow-on threats.
coas.reserve_posture: 8 aircraft remain: 2 at Base North, 2 at Highridge (1 fighter + 1 UAV), 2 at Southern Redoubt, 2 at Spear Point. SAM-Eastern available.
coas.estimated_outcome: Layered response — fighters and SAM both covering the primary axis. Provides depth: if fighters miss, SAM is fallback. UAV forward screen improves early warning for wave 2. Moderate commitment.
assumptions: trk-h01 enters both fighter and SAM engagement envelopes
UAV can reach forward position in time to detect follow-on tracks
Single fighter adequate for trk-h02 given SAM backup potential
coas.rationale: Combines the strengths of options A and B: fighters provide high-probability intercept on the primary threat, SAM provides depth and fallback, and the forward UAV addresses the key unknown — whether a second wave is coming. The trade-off is committing a UAV and reducing Highridge's local reserve to one fighter. Ranked third because it splits fighter effort across two bases and relies on a single aircraft for the eastern axis.
